parts.prompt:
A student combusts a sample of ascorbic acid, x y x, to determine its chemical
CO $H_2O$
composition. The only products of the reaction are 0.2400 mol of 2 and 2.883 g of 2 .
$H_2O$
i. Calculate the number of moles of 2 produced.
ii. The mole ratio of carbon (C) to oxygen (O) is 1:1 in ascorbic acid. Based on this
information and your answer to part A (i), determine the empirical formula of ascorbic
acid.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (a)(i): moles of H₂O = 2.883 g ÷ 18.02 g/mol = 0.1600 mol. Part (a)(ii): empirical formula is C₃H₄O₃ (or accept CHO if not fully reduced — but C₃H₄O₃ is the correct empirical formula for ascorbic acid given C:O = 1:1 and 0.2400 mol C and 0.3200 mol H atoms).

scoring_points.description: For the correct empirical formula consistent with the combustion data: 1 point

required_elements: Moles of H₂O = $\frac{2.883\text{ g}}{18.02\text{ g/mol}} = 0.1600$ mol
Moles of H = $2 \times 0.1600 = 0.3200$ mol; moles of C = 0.2400 mol; moles of O = 0.2400 mol (given C:O = 1:1)
alternatives.correct_example:
$n(\text{H}_2\text{O}) = \frac{2.883\text{ g}}{18.02\text{ g/mol}} = 0.1600\text{ mol}$ 
$n(\text{H}) = 2 \times 0.1600 = 0.3200\text{ mol}$ 
$n(\text{C}) = 0.2400\text{ mol}$; since C:O = 1:1, $n(\text{O}) = 0.2400\text{ mol}$ 
Ratio: C : H : O = 0.2400 : 0.3200 : 0.2400 = 3 : 4 : 3 
Empirical formula: $\text{C}_3\text{H}_4\text{O}_3$

wrong_examples: Using moles of CO₂ directly as moles of H₂O.
Forgetting that each mole of H₂O contains 2 moles of H atoms.
common_traps: Not applying the C:O = 1:1 constraint to determine oxygen content separately.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Points 01–03: Point 01: For the correct calculated value: $2.883\text{ g H}_2\text{O} \times \frac{1\text{ mol H}_2\text{O}}{18.02\text{ g H}_2\text{O}} = 0.1600\text{ mol H}_2\text{O}$. Point 02: For the correct calculated number of moles of H (may be implicit): $0.1600\text{ mol H}_2\text{O} \times \frac{2\text{ mol H}}{1\text{ mol H}_2\text{O}} = 0.3200\text{ mol H}$. Point 03: For the correct empirical formula: C₃H₄O₃.

sample_responses.response: $n(\text{H}_2\text{O}) = \frac{2.883\text{ g}}{18.02\text{ g/mol}} = 0.1600\text{ mol}$; $n(\text{H}) = 2 \times 0.1600 = 0.3200\text{ mol}$. Since C:O = 1:1 and $n(\text{C}) = 0.2400\text{ mol CO}_2 = 0.2400\text{ mol C}$, then $n(\text{O}) = 0.2400\text{ mol}$. Ratio C:H:O = 0.2400:0.3200:0.2400 = 3:4:3. Empirical formula: $\text{C}_3\text{H}_4\text{O}_3$.
sample_responses.commentary: Calculates moles of H₂O, derives moles of H correctly (×2), uses the C:O = 1:1 constraint, and correctly simplifies to the 3:4:3 ratio. Earns all three sub-points.

sample_responses.response: $n(\text{H}_2\text{O}) = 0.1600\text{ mol}$; $n(\text{H}) = 0.1600\text{ mol}$ (forgot the factor of 2). Ratio becomes C:H:O = 0.2400:0.1600:0.2400 = 3:2:3. Empirical formula: $\text{C}_3\text{H}_2\text{O}_3$.
sample_responses.commentary: Forgot that each mole of H₂O contains 2 moles of H atoms. The H count must be doubled. This is a common error that gives an incorrect empirical formula. No credit for the empirical formula point.

parts.prompt:
Ascorbic acid,
HAsc(aq),
acts as a weak acid, as shown in the equation.
HAsc(aq)+ $H_2O$(l) E $H_2O$+(aq)+ Asc-(aq)
The following titration curve was produced when a 10.0 mL sample of
HAsc(aq)
was titrated
using
0.0550
M
NaOH(aq).
i. Calculate the molar concentration of the ascorbic acid solution.
ii. From the titration curve, determine the approximate $pK_a$ of ascorbic acid.
Asc-
iii. What is the value of the ratio 7 A when the pH of the solution is 4.7?
HAsc

DHAsc
parts.scoring_notes: Part (b)(i): equivalence point volume read from graph ≈ 8.0 mL; M(HAsc) = (0.0550)(0.008)/(0.010). Part (b)(ii): pKa ≈ pH at half-equivalence point (4 mL on graph) ≈ 4.7. Part (b)(iii): when pH = pKa, [Asc⁻]/[HAsc] = 1.

scoring_points.description: For correct answers to (b)(i), (b)(ii), and (b)(iii) consistent with the graph: 1 point

required_elements: (i) $[\text{HAsc}] = \frac{(0.0550\text{ M})(V_{eq})}{0.0100\text{ L}}$ where $V_{eq}$ is read from the graph.
alternatives.correct_example:
(i) At equivalence, $n(\text{NaOH}) = n(\text{HAsc})$: $[\text{HAsc}] = \frac{(0.0550)(0.0080)}{0.0100} = 0.044\text{ M}$ 
(ii) p$K_a \approx 4.7$ (pH at half-equivalence point from graph) 
(iii) Henderson–Hasselbalch: $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\frac{[\text{Asc}^-]}{[\text{HAsc}]}$; when pH = 4.7 = p$K_a$, $\log\frac{[\text{Asc}^-]}{[\text{HAsc}]} = 0$, so ratio = 1.

wrong_examples: Reading the equivalence point volume incorrectly from the graph.
Reporting the ratio as 0 instead of 1 when pH = pKa.
common_traps: Using pH at the equivalence point rather than the half-equivalence point to find pKa.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Points 04–06: Point 04: For the correct calculated value of [HAsc]: $0.0160\text{ L NaOH} \times \frac{0.0550\text{ mol NaOH}}{1\text{ L}} \times \frac{1\text{ mol HAsc}}{1\text{ mol NaOH}} = 8.80 \times 10^{-4}\text{ mol HAsc}$; $\frac{8.80 \times 10^{-4}\text{ mol}}{0.0100\text{ L}} = 0.0880\text{ M HAsc}$. Point 05: For the correct pKa: 4.1 (acceptable range: 4.0–4.3). Point 06: For the correct ratio $[\text{Asc}^-]/[\text{HAsc}] = 4.0$, consistent with part B(ii) using Henderson–Hasselbalch.

sample_responses.response: (i) At the equivalence point (16 mL NaOH from graph): $n(\text{HAsc}) = (0.0550)(0.0160) = 8.80 \times 10^{-4}\text{ mol}$; $[\text{HAsc}] = 8.80 \times 10^{-4} / 0.0100 = 0.0880\text{ M}$. (ii) At the half-equivalence point (8 mL NaOH), pH ≈ 4.1 = pKa. (iii) pH = 4.7 = pKa + log([Asc⁻]/[HAsc]); $4.7 = 4.1 + \log([\text{Asc}^-]/[\text{HAsc}])$; ratio = $10^{0.6} \approx 4.0$.
sample_responses.commentary: Reads the equivalence volume from the graph, correctly calculates [HAsc], identifies pKa at the half-equivalence point, and applies Henderson–Hasselbalch to find the ratio. Earns all three sub-points.

sample_responses.response: (ii) pKa = pH at the equivalence point ≈ 8.5.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses the pH at the equivalence point instead of the half-equivalence point. The equivalence point pH indicates full neutralization (buffer capacity is zero); pKa is read at the half-equivalence point where [HA] = [A⁻]. No credit for this sub-point.

Dehydroascorbic acid ( ) can be produced by reacting ascorbic acid with the triiodide
-
ion, I 3 , as represented by the following equation.
HAsc+ I - " DHAsc+ 3I-+ 2 H+
HAsc
The student runs three trials of the reaction with different initial concentrations of and
-
I 3 , producing the following data.

1 0.450 1.200

4.914× 10$^{-4}$
i. The rate law for the reaction is rate = k HAsc I 3 - . Explain how the data in the table
7 A7 A
HAsc
support the conclusion that the reaction is first order with respect to .
ii. Calculate the value of the rate constant, k, for the reaction. Include units with your
answer.
-
parts.scoring_notes: Part (c)(i): compare trials 1 and 3 (same [I₃⁻], [HAsc] doubles, rate doubles → first order in HAsc). Part (c)(ii): use any trial; k = rate/([HAsc][I₃⁻]); units = M⁻¹s⁻¹.

scoring_points.description: For a correct explanation of first-order dependence on HAsc using data: 1 point

required_elements: Comparing trials 1 and 3: [I₃⁻] is constant; [HAsc] doubles from 0.450 to 0.900 M; rate also doubles from 2.457×10⁻⁴ to 4.914×10⁻⁴ M/s.
A doubling of [HAsc] that doubles the rate is consistent with first-order dependence on HAsc.
alternatives.correct_example: Comparing trials 1 and 3: $[\text{I}_3^-]$ is held constant at 1.200 M while $[\text{HAsc}]$ doubles (0.450 → 0.900 M). The rate also doubles ($2.457 \times 10^{-4}$ → $4.914 \times 10^{-4}$ M/s). Since doubling the concentration of HAsc doubles the rate, the reaction is first order with respect to HAsc.

wrong_examples: Comparing trials 1 and 2 to argue for HAsc order — those trials vary I₃⁻, not HAsc.
common_traps: Using the wrong pair of trials; to isolate the HAsc order, must hold [I₃⁻] constant.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point 07: For a correct explanation: $\frac{4.914 \times 10^{-4}}{2.457 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.900}{0.450} = 2$, thus $a = 1$. OR: Comparing trials 1 and 3, the rate doubles when the concentration of ascorbic acid is doubled and the triiodide ion concentration is constant, indicating that the process is first order with respect to ascorbic acid.

sample_responses.response: Comparing trials 1 and 3: $[\text{I}_3^-]$ is constant at 1.200 M. When [HAsc] doubles from 0.450 M to 0.900 M, the rate doubles from $2.457 \times 10^{-4}$ to $4.914 \times 10^{-4}$ M/s. Since doubling [HAsc] doubles the rate, the reaction is first order with respect to HAsc.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly holds [I₃⁻] constant, identifies the doubling of both [HAsc] and rate, and draws the first-order conclusion. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Comparing trials 1 and 2: [HAsc] is the same, but [I₃⁻] halves and the rate halves, showing first order in HAsc.
sample_responses.commentary: Trials 1 and 2 vary [I₃⁻], not [HAsc]. This comparison demonstrates the order with respect to I₃⁻, not HAsc. Must compare trials 1 and 3 to isolate the HAsc dependence. No credit.

scoring_points.description: For the correct numerical value of k with correct units (M⁻¹s⁻¹): 1 point
wrong_examples: Reporting units as s⁻¹ (first-order units) for a second-order rate constant.
Dividing rate by only one concentration.
common_traps: For a second-order overall rate law (first order in each reactant), units of k are M⁻¹s⁻¹.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Points 08–09: Point 08: For the correct calculated value: $k = \frac{\text{rate}}{[\text{HAsc}][\text{I}_3^-]} = \frac{2.457 \times 10^{-4}\text{ M/s}}{(0.450\text{ M})(1.200\text{ M})} = 4.55 \times 10^{-4}\text{ M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$. Point 09: For the correct units: M⁻¹s⁻¹.

sample_responses.response: Using trial 1: $k = \frac{2.457 \times 10^{-4}\text{ M/s}}{(0.450\text{ M})(1.200\text{ M})} = 4.55 \times 10^{-4}\text{ M}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly divides the rate by both concentrations and reports the units as M⁻¹s⁻¹, appropriate for a second-order overall rate law. Earns both sub-points.
Divides by only one concentration (HAsc) instead of both [HAsc] and [I₃⁻]. This gives first-order units (s⁻¹) and the wrong numerical value. No credit.

parts.prompt:
The triiodide ion, I 3 , is significantly more soluble in water than elemental iodine, $I_2$, is.
-
Identify an intermolecular force between I 3 and water that is not present between $I_2$ and
-
water, which could explain the difference in solubility. Lewis diagrams for $I_2$ and I 3 are
provided.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept 'ion–dipole' or 'ion-dipole interactions/attractions' only. Do not accept hydrogen bonding.

scoring_points.description: For identifying ion–dipole attractions as the intermolecular force unique to I₃⁻/water: 1 point

required_elements: Ion–dipole attractions exist between I₃⁻ (an ion) and the polar water molecules.
I₂ is a nonpolar molecule and can only interact with water via weak London dispersion forces, not ion–dipole.
alternatives.correct_example: Ion–dipole attractions are present between $\text{I}_3^-$ ions and the polar water molecules but are not present between nonpolar $\text{I}_2$ molecules and water. Ion–dipole forces are much stronger than the dispersion forces that $\text{I}_2$ can exert, explaining the greater solubility of $\text{I}_3^-$.

wrong_examples: Stating hydrogen bonding — neither I₃⁻ nor I₂ can form hydrogen bonds with water through the halogen.
Stating dipole–dipole — I₃⁻ is an ion, not a polar molecule.
common_traps: Confusing ion–dipole with dipole–dipole; the former requires at least one ion.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Point 10: For the correct answer: Ion-dipole attractions are present between I₃⁻ ions and water but not between I₂ molecules and water.

sample_responses.response: Ion–dipole attractions exist between \ce{I3-} (an anion) and the polar water molecules. Since \ce{I2} is a nonpolar molecule, it cannot form ion–dipole interactions with water — only weak London dispersion forces exist between \ce{I2} and water.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies ion–dipole as the force unique to I₃⁻/water interactions and explains why it is absent for the nonpolar I₂ molecule. Matches CB's stated rubric. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: \ce{I3-} forms hydrogen bonds with water because it is negatively charged.
sample_responses.commentary: Hydrogen bonding requires N–H, O–H, or F–H as a donor and N, O, or F as acceptor. Iodine does not qualify. The correct force is ion–dipole, not hydrogen bonding. No credit.
